us-politics.headline: U.S. Military Rescues Airman Shot Down Over Iran
us-politics.body:
The U.S. military has recovered an American airman who was shot down over Iran, President Trump announced. Few operational details have been released, but the rescue marks a rare successful military extraction from Iranian territory. The incident raises immediate questions about the scope of U.S. military activity near or over Iran and what escalation risks follow.

The White House has not yet provided a full account of the mission, and independent verification remains limited. The story is developing.

sources.title: Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran

us-politics.headline: Judge Blocks Trump Push on College Admissions Race Data
us-politics.body:
A federal judge has blocked a Trump administration effort to require colleges to certify that race played no role in admissions decisions. The ruling is a setback for the administration's broader campaign to eliminate race-conscious practices in higher education following the Supreme Court's 2023 affirmative action ruling.

Supporters of the administration's move argue colleges have been slow to comply with the Supreme Court decision. Critics say the certification requirement was an overreach that threatened federal funding as a coercive tool. The legal fight over campus admissions policy is far from over.

sources.title: Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions

us-politics.headline: DHS and Van Hollen Clash Over ICE Pursuit Crash
us-politics.body:
The Department of Homeland Security is pushing back against Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen, who claimed an illegal immigrant caused a crash while fleeing ICE agents. DHS says the facts support its account and accuses Van Hollen of misrepresenting the incident to undermine immigration enforcement.

Van Hollen has disputed the DHS framing, arguing the pursuit itself created dangerous conditions. The dispute reflects a wider pattern of sharp disagreement between the administration and Democratic lawmakers over ICE tactics and accountability. Neither side has released full documentation publicly.

sources.title: DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE

us-politics.headline: Vehicle Hits Louisiana Parade Crowd; Congress Eyes Recess Rules
us-politics.body:
Several people were injured after a vehicle drove into a crowd at a Louisiana parade. Authorities are investigating the incident, and no motive has been confirmed. The event follows a series of vehicle-ramming incidents at public gatherings across the country in recent years.

Separately, Washington has been focused on the mechanics of pro forma congressional sessions after a prolonged DHS funding dispute stretched through a congressional recess. Pro forma sessions prevent the president from making recess appointments, and both parties have used them strategically. The procedural standoff highlights ongoing tension between the executive branch and Congress over appointments and spending authority.

sources.title: Several injured after vehicle drives into crowd at Louisiana parade
A de facto pro forma: Why Washington fixated on these sessions as the DHS shutdown dragged on through a recess

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid shortage fears
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian nations have assured Australia that fuel supplies will continue normally, as Prime Minister Albanese works to prevent shortages from escalating. The diplomatic reassurances come as the government faces pressure over energy security and cost-of-living pressures tied to fuel prices.

On the domestic front, nearly 100 NSW service stations have been fined $1,100 each for displaying misleading petrol prices during the crunch. Western Australia is also expanding its FuelWatch scheme, requiring all regional stations to report prices from May. Farmers are among those hit hardest, with some saying they are struggling to put food on the table as fuel costs bite into already thin margins.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands

au-politics.headline: Sisters of slain officer to sue Victoria Police for negligence
au-politics.body:
The sisters of Senior Constable Neal Thompson, killed by Dezi Freeman, plan to sue Victoria Police for negligence. Their lawyer says the family feels abandoned by the force and believes systemic failures contributed to Thompson's death.

The case raises pointed questions about police duty of care to their own officers. Victoria Police has not yet responded publicly to the legal action. The lawsuit is likely to intensify scrutiny of how the force manages risk and supports officers in dangerous situations.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says
'Abandoned': Sisters of officer slain by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: Murder arrest at Melbourne airport; Tasmania Police shooting
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport while attempting to board an international flight, and has since been charged with murdering a woman. Police intercepted him before he could leave the country.

Separately, a man is in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police in Goodwood, southern Tasmania. Details of the circumstances remain limited. Both incidents add to a busy Easter weekend for Australian law enforcement.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police

au-politics.headline: Seven-year-old girl dies in Adelaide ATV rollover
au-politics.body:
A seven-year-old girl has died following an ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east, in one of two separate fatal crashes reported on Easter Sunday. The incidents have renewed concerns about ATV safety, particularly for children.

ATV-related deaths remain a persistent problem in Australia, with advocates long calling for tighter regulations on who can operate the vehicles and under what conditions. Authorities have not yet released full details of either crash.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east

world.headline: US-Iran War Enters Day 37 as Conflict Spreads
world.body:
Now 37 days into US-Israeli strikes on Iran, the conflict is spilling well beyond Iranian borders. Iran struck Kuwait's power and water infrastructure, continuing a pattern of attacks on Gulf states, while Tehran rejected Trump's latest ultimatum and claimed to have downed another US aircraft. A Hormuz deadline looms, though Iran signalled it would allow Iraqi ships to pass the strait - a narrow carve-out that underscores how the waterway has become a pressure point.

Trump, facing domestic polling that shows the war is deeply unpopular, is reportedly seeking an exit that avoids the appearance of failure. Thousands rallied in Baghdad against the campaign. US satellite firm Planet Labs announced a blackout on imagery of the conflict zone, raising concerns about information access.

sources.title: Trump seeks to end the war without it becoming ‘an abject failure’
Iran war: What is happening on day 37 of US-Israeli attacks?
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images
Iran war live: Iran claims new downing of US plane, Hormuz ‘deadline’ looms
Thousands rally in Iraq against ‘senseless’ US-Israel war on Iran
Kuwait’s power, water plants damaged as Iran keeps attacking Gulf states

world.headline: US Rescues Second Downed Airman from Inside Iran
world.body:
American forces rescued a second pilot whose F-15 was shot down over Iran, with Trump calling the operation 'miraculous.' The rescue marks a rare successful extraction from deep inside hostile territory and follows an earlier recovery of a downed airman. Details on how the mission was executed remain limited, with the Pentagon releasing little beyond confirmation.

The rescues highlight both the intensity of air operations over Iran and the risks US pilots are running. Iran's air defences have now claimed multiple American aircraft, and the downed-pilot problem has become a recurring operational challenge for US commanders.

sources.title: What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms

world.headline: Ukraine Drones Strike Russian Oil Targets Deep Inside Russia
world.body:
Ukrainian drones hit a port in Primorsk and an oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod, sparking fires and reported fuel leaks. The Nizhny Novgorod strike is notable for its reach - the city sits roughly 400 kilometres east of Moscow - and signals a continued Ukrainian strategy of targeting Russian energy infrastructure to strain the war economy.

The attacks come as global attention remains heavily focused on the Iran conflict, but Kyiv is pressing its long-range campaign regardless. Russia has not yet issued a formal response to the latest strikes.

sources.title: Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod

world.headline: Houthis Hit Ben Gurion; Palestinian Christians Face Quiet Holy Week
world.body:
Yemen's Houthis claimed a cluster missile attack on Israel's Ben Gurion airport, the latest in a series of strikes the group has launched in solidarity with Iran and Gaza. Israel's air defences have intercepted most previous Houthi missiles, though the group's persistence keeps pressure on Israeli civilian infrastructure.

Inside the occupied territories, Palestinian Christians marked Holy Week under tight Israeli restrictions, with access to Jerusalem's holy sites severely curtailed. In Hebron, Israeli measures continued to tighten control over the Ibrahimi Mosque. The combination of war, blockade and movement restrictions has left one of Christianity's most sacred seasons almost unrecognisable for those living it.

sources.title: Yemen’s Houthis claim cluster missile attack on Israel’s Ben Gurion airport
Under Israeli restrictions, Palestinian Christians mark quiet Holy Week
Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque

ai.headline: Claude Code Leak Spawns Malware, Anthropic Adds Pricing Friction
ai.body:
Anthropic is having a rough week. Hackers are distributing what they claim is leaked Claude Code source, bundling it with malware to catch developers who download it carelessly. The incident highlights the security risks that follow high-profile AI tool releases.

Meanwhile, Anthropic confirmed that Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra to use OpenClaw, its new agentic capability, on top of existing subscription fees. The move signals a broader industry shift toward metered pricing for compute-heavy AI features. Users who assumed their subscriptions covered the full product are finding the goalposts have moved.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage

ai.headline: AI Fakes, Copyright Trolls, and the Authenticity Arms Race
ai.body:
A folk musician found herself targeted by AI-generated impersonations and a copyright troll simultaneously, illustrating how the same legal and technical systems meant to protect creators can be weaponised against them. The case is a sharp example of how AI fakes create openings for bad actors beyond the obvious reputational damage.

The backlash is pushing creators and platforms toward provenance signals. The Verge reports growing demand for "human-made" labels on creative work, with designers and artists seeking ways to certify AI-free output. Whether such labels will carry legal or commercial weight remains unclear, but the market pressure is real and accelerating.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Right-to-Repair and eIDAS Show Tech Policy Battles Intensifying
ai.body:
Colorado passed one of the strongest right-to-repair laws in the US, and tech companies are already working to hollow it out. Lobbyists are pushing amendments that would carve out software and firmware from repair protections, which would gut the law's practical effect on modern devices where software locks are the primary barrier.

In Europe, Germany's eIDAS digital identity implementation has drawn criticism for requiring users to hold an Apple or Google account to function. That dependency hands two American platform companies a chokepoint over a sovereign digital identity system. Both stories reflect the same pattern: legislation advances, then industry finds the seams.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law
German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Cuts PostgreSQL Performance in Half on AWS
ai.body:
An AWS engineer has documented a severe PostgreSQL performance regression under Linux 7.0, with throughput dropping by roughly half in some workloads. The cause appears to be changes in memory management and scheduler behaviour introduced in the new kernel, and a straightforward fix is not yet in sight.

For cloud operators, the timing is painful. Linux 7.0 is already rolling out across managed environments, and database workloads are among the most latency-sensitive in production infrastructure. AWS and other cloud providers will face pressure to either delay kernel upgrades or absorb the performance hit while upstream maintainers work toward a resolution.

sources.title: AWS engineer reports PostgreSQL perf halved by Linux 7.0, fix may not be easy

btc.headline: Bitcoin Sentiment Turns Bearish as Quantum Threat Looms
btc.body:
Bearish chatter about Bitcoin on social media hit a five-week high this week, according to Santiment data, as price stagnation frustrates traders. Historically, elevated crowd pessimism has preceded recoveries, but the current mood reflects genuine uncertainty rather than simple impatience.

Underneath the sentiment noise, a longer-term threat is drawing serious attention. With Bitcoin's market cap sitting at $1.3 trillion, researchers and developers are racing to harden the network against quantum computing attacks. Key initiatives are targeting cryptographic upgrades that would protect wallets and transactions from future quantum-capable adversaries. The window to act is uncertain, but the stakes are not.

sources.title: Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment
Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

btc.headline: Drift Protocol Hack Was Months in the Making
btc.body:
The $280 million exploit of Drift Protocol was not opportunistic. Preliminary findings show attackers spent months preparing the attack, mapping vulnerabilities and timing their move carefully. That level of deliberate planning puts it in a different category from typical DeFi hacks.

The findings raise hard questions about protocol auditing and on-chain monitoring. If the groundwork was laid over months, detection should have been possible. The incident adds to a growing list of large-scale DeFi exploits that expose the gap between code deployment and ongoing security vigilance.

sources.title: Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Anthropic Enters Politics as AI Policy Battle Heats Up
btc.body:
Anthropic has launched a political action committee, stepping directly into Washington's AI policy fight at a moment of rising tension with the Trump administration. The move signals that major AI labs are no longer willing to leave regulatory outcomes to chance.

For crypto markets, the development is a reminder that the regulatory environment shaping AI will increasingly intersect with blockchain and digital asset policy. Anthropic's PAC entry follows a broader pattern of tech companies building political infrastructure to influence rules before they are written.

sources.title: Anthropic launches PAC amid tensions with Trump administration over AI policy

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form savory tart with caramelized leeks and shiitake mushrooms lacquered in white miso butter on a flaky rough-puff pastry base.
food.tip: Chill your butter in the freezer for 15 minutes before making the pastry and handle the dough as little as possible — visible butter streaks mean proper flakiness.

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that builds through four 8-minute blocks at progressively faster paces — easy, moderate, comfortably hard, and back to moderate — training your body to recover at speed rather than at rest.

learn.topic: Eigenvalues and the Google PageRank Algorithm
learn.description: PageRank, the algorithm that made Google dominant, is essentially an application of a linear algebra concept called the dominant eigenvector — it treats the entire web as a matrix and finds which pages the system naturally 'settles on' as important.
learn.why: Understanding this reveals that one of the most consequential pieces of software ever written is, at its core, a piece of 19th-century mathematics, and it opens a door into how machine learning, quantum computing, and network theory all share the same hidden skeleton.
